message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full
**Title:** Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich et al., Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013)
**Found via:** training and adaptation in blind listeners search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind humans. Specifically: (1) What playback/speech rates do trained blind listeners comprehend (in words per minute or syllables per second, and any comparison to normal speech rate)? (2) What is the perceptual limit for sighted/untrained listeners vs trained blind listeners? (3) What brain regions are involved and how does training/adaptation reorganize them? (4) Any details on training effects, adaptation, or signal processing/time-compression methods used. Provide exact quotes with the relevant numbers and rates. Also provide the publication date.

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Humans

**Publication Date:** October 23, 2013

## Speech Rate Comprehension Levels

**Trained Blind Listeners:**
- Can understand speech at approximately 18 syllables per second (syl/s)
- Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second"
- Achieved ~70% accuracy after 6-month training period

**Sighted/Untrained Listeners:**
- Maximum comprehension: ~8 syl/s
- Quote: "the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"
- Cannot effectively process ultra-fast rates

**Performance Comparison:**
The trained blind group exceeded sighted performance by roughly 2.25x (18 vs 8 syl/s).

## Brain Regions Involved

Training induced reorganization in:
- **Right primary visual cortex (V1)** and secondary visual areas in subjects with very low residual vision
- **Left supplementary motor area (SMA)** - timing operations for syllable structure
- **Left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG)** - phonological processing
- **Right temporal pole** - in sighted participants (semantic processing)

Quote on visual system recruitment: "right-hemispheric visual system (V1, Pv) was, apparently, recruited indicating functional reorganization at the level of the occipital lobes"

## Key Training Finding

Quote: "Four of the six participants showed...considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)"

content.text: Let me get more detail on the specific numbers, the normal speech rate baseline, and the mechanism, to firm up the falsifiable claims.

input.prompt: Find exact quotes and numbers for: (1) the normal/conversational speech rate baseline in syllables per second mentioned in the paper; (2) the highest rate of ultra-fast speech that blind experts can comprehend (up to ~22 syl/s?) and how that compares to normal speech; (3) the specific role of the fusiform gyrus or right visual cortex (V1) activation correlating with ultra-fast speech comprehension performance; (4) whether the study used screen reader / synthetic speech / time-compression. Give verbatim quotes. Also note the journal name and authors.

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Study: Key Findings

**Journal & Authors:**
*Frontiers in Human Neuroscience* (2013); Dietrich, Hertrich, and Ackermann

---

## (1) Normal Conversational Speech Rate Baseline

"whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s" (normal sighted listeners' maximum comprehension rate)

---

## (2) Ultra-Fast Speech Rate for Blind Experts

"vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners"

This represents a **2.75x increase** over normal speech comprehension limits.

---

## (3) Visual Cortex Activation & Performance

Right visual cortex activation correlated with comprehension capability:

"blind people highly skilled in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar"

The fusiform gyrus appeared to support phonological operations: "left FG as a secondary phonological area might expand the phonological network and help to cope with the higher processing demands"

---

## (4) Speech Presentation Method

The study used **screen-reader synthetic speech**:

"participants were instructed to use the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence')"

Speech was presented "at the three speaking rates considered (30 stimuli each at 8, 13, or 18 syl/s)"

content.text: I now have sufficient detail to extract the claims.

claims.claim: Trained late-blind individuals can comprehend ultra-fast synthetic (screen-reader) speech at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, far exceeding sighted listeners' capacities.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners

claims.claim: The maximum comprehension rate for normal/untrained sighted listeners is around 8 syllables per second, establishing the perceptual ceiling for non-experts.
claims.quote: the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)

claims.claim: Blind experts highly skilled in ultra-fast speech show hemodynamic activation of right primary visual cortex (V1), fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar, indicating functional reorganization of the central-visual system supports accelerated speech comprehension.
claims.quote: blind people highly skilled in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar

claims.claim: Comprehension of ultra-fast speech is a trainable skill: after a roughly 6-month training period, four of six participants reached about 70% correctly repeated words.
claims.quote: Four of the six participants showed...considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)

claims.claim: The study delivered accelerated speech via the JAWS screen reader using the synthetic Eloquence voice at fixed rates of 8, 13, and 18 syllables per second, demonstrating that time-compressed synthetic speech is the medium for extreme speed listening.
claims.quote: participants were instructed to use the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence')

content.text: Extracted claims from the Dietrich et al. (2013) Frontiers in Human Neuroscience primary research article. Key findings bear directly on the perceptual limits and training/adaptation aspects of the research question: trained late-blind listeners comprehend ultra-fast speech up to ~22 syl/s versus ~8 syl/s for untrained sighted listeners, this capacity is trainable (~70% accuracy after ~6 months), it correlates with functional reorganization recruiting right visual cortex (V1), fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar, and the speech was delivered via JAWS/Eloquence synthetic time-compressed speech. Source rated primary (peer-reviewed neuroscience journal). Note: the study tested rates up to 18 syl/s and references experts reaching ~22 syl/s — the research question's "16x" framing maps to this syllable-rate domain rather than a literal multiplier, since these are absolute syllable rates roughly 2.25-2.75x normal comprehension limits.
